solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int lengthAfterTransformations(String s, int t, List<Integer> nums) {
        final int MOD = 1000000007;
        int[] charCount = new int[26];
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            charCount[c - 'a']++;
        }

        int[][] next = new int[26][26];
        for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            int steps = nums.get(i);
            for (int j = 0; j < 26; j++) {
                int nextChar = (i + steps) % 26;
                next[i][j] = nextChar;
            }
        }

        int[] current = new int[26];
        for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            current[i] = charCount[i];
        }

        for (int trans = 0; trans < t; trans++) {
            int[] nextCount = new int[26];
            for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
                if (current[i] == 0) continue;
                int steps = nums.get(i);
                int nextChar = (i + steps) % 26;
                nextCount[nextChar] = (nextCount[nextChar] + current[i]) % MOD;
            }
            current = nextCount;
        }

        long result = 0;
        for (int count : current) {
            result = (result + count) % MOD;
        }

        return (int) result;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(t * 26)
space complexity: O(26)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a dynamic programming approach to track the count of each character after each transformation. Instead of simulating the string directly, it maintains an array of counts for each character. For each transformation, it calculates the new count of each character based on the transformation rules. This avoids the need to process the entire string repeatedly, making the solution efficient even for large values of t.
